Okay, now that you understand how to start generating lead flow for businesses that are for sale, we're going to discuss investigating and qualifying those leads. If you're looking for distressed businesses that are not profitable, they're in debt, they're struggling, there's some red flags you want to think about. First and foremost, have they had declining revenues over a long period of time? Secondly, have they had declining profitability over a long period of time? Third, you want to make sure do they have key customers that are starting to go away? You want to look at churn rate, how many new customers come in and how many new customers leave because that will tell you if you have poor operations. The other things you want to look for are things like do they continue to lose money as they're trying to scale and grow? Do they have key employees in them that are maybe set up their own side business and they're not focused on making the business successful? There's a lot of red flags when businesses don't work and what you need to be aware of is what some of those red flags are. However, if you're looking to buy a business that's already cash flowing, it's hot, it's working, it's the kind of business that you want, leads are being generated, sales are happening, it's got a team in place. Now, then what you should be doing is looking for businesses that are earning 15 to 25% pre-tax margin while they can pay you the owner between $100,000 and $250,000 annually for the effort and energy you want to contribute to the business. And if that business has the potential to earn an additional 10 or 20% from profitability, this will really help you if you're looking for a loan. The business should already have recurring revenue from existing clients or you should be able to see that you can create this within the business. Recurring revenue is one of the most stable strategies to sustain business growth and is easily the single largest way to create value in a business. Any business with high service revenues that repeat over and over and over that give you the opportunity to sell something new to those people later, those are remarkable businesses. So thin and wide is very dangerous, lots of transactions at little tiny dollars because there's no scale in that. Also long or deep and narrow are very dangerous because you might have a handful of clients and if you lose them, the business is in big trouble.
